posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked:
Hi,

I have a bottle of 2001 and 2006 Rayas standing up. Which one should I open tonight? Anyone have an opinion on which is drinking better at the moment?

Any suggestions on preparation? I’m thinking a double decant back into bottle a few hours before dinner?

Thanks,

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked:
Johathan,

I’ve never had the 2006 but I did have a sensational bottle of the 2001 this past August. One of the best wines I’ve ever had and it fully justified the exalted reputation of this estate.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: Had the 01 in June, extraordinary wine. The nose was particularly explosive

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The 2001 has begun to get tertiary and complex (and it’s been a year since I had it), whereas the 2006 is still pretty boisterous and exuberantly fruity; more powerful too, though the 01 is not a weed either.

Either way, I strongly recommend following the Reynaud opening protocol: pull the cork now, pour off a small glass, replace the cork and return to the cellar until dinner time.

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked:
c fu:

Had the 01 in June, extraordinary wine. The nose was particularly explosive

Absolutely explosive, about the most intriguing (and come hither) wine I’ve ever smelled. And the palate matched the bouquet. A great, great wine, and sadly my only bottle .

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Jonathan H:

Hi,

I have a bottle of 2001 and 2006 Rayas standing up. Which one should I open tonight? Anyone have an opinion on which is drinking better at the moment?

Any suggestions on preparation? I’m thinking a double decant back into bottle a few hours before dinner?

Thanks,

2001!
Open as early as possible (8-12+ h) … Emmanuel Reynaud recommends a day or two …
Decant only 15-20 min. before serving … (and softly, not double …)
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: 2001 is sensational
If you ask to Emmanuel Reynaud… He would say that both are way too young!!!

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked: No wine is too young if we’re too old!

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
PhilippeCamps:

If you ask to Emmanuel Reynaud… He would say that both are way too young!!!

yes, he ( and I) would recommend the 1997 … but when it is not available … what can one do?

BTW: had 2000 R. recently, far far better ( as usual) than described (by RMP), but 2001 and also 1999 is better, more intense, longer.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: Goes to show how drop dead gorgeous Grenache can be . . . And the Pignan is not a slouch either.

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:

BTW: had 2000 R. recently, far far better ( as usual) than described (by RMP), but 2001 and also 1999 is better, more intense, longer.

Nice to see your positive thoughts on the '99 as it’s my only remaining bottle of Rayas. I’ve had it once and liked it a good deal but that was a number of years ago and at the time it hadn’t become transcendent like I found the 2001.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Frank Drew:
Gerhard P.:

BTW: had 2000 R. recently, far far better ( as usual) than described (by RMP), but 2001 and also 1999 is better, more intense, longer.

Nice to see your positive thoughts on the '99 as it’s my only remaining bottle of Rayas. I’ve had it once and liked it a good deal but that was a number of years ago and at the time it hadn’t become transcendent like I found the 2001.

Generally: Rayas (and Pignan) is not a wine to PnPour … it needs a lot ( a LOT) of air …
and also if it is enjoyable quite young it unfolds all the complexity and magic only with age …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Al1 M ï c h ě l
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:
Alain M:

^ From the TNs I’ve read, 2000 seems like the weaker vintage in the 1998-20xx.

+1 on the 2001, the 2006 is still too young imo.

Alain

Weeeeelllll … TNs

A slightly lesser vintage of Rayas is still much more interesting than 95% of the rest of CdP.

Possibly, but the TNs I’ve read on this vintage point pretty much all in the same direction, ie. that 2000 is not a great vintage. I’ve read this from people who know and drink Rayas on the regular, and who have a palate similar to mine.
It is the only vintage in the 20xx that I did not buy. I may buy some if Emmanuel Reynaud proposes me some for my next order, but not at the price it goes for at wine shops.

Alain
posts.display_username: Al1 M ï c h ě l

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: So what’d you open

posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked:
I actually ended up opening both I have a few more of the 2006 so I thought “why not?”, curiously got the best of me and I couldn’t resist. I find it informative and interesting to see how a wine develops over time, so I didn’t feel too bad opening the 2006 in its youth.

Both wines were phenomenal!! The 2006 is admittedly young, but it still showed great. The aromatics were gorgeous. A friend who is big into Burgundy commented that there was something about the 2006 that reminded him of Jacky Truchot’s wines. On the palate, it really was like nothing else I have tasted before. This was until I tried the 2001. WOW… it was definitely a wine highlight for me. Something I don’t think I will forget for the rest of my life. It was like the 2006, but on steroids. Everything was just so much more pronounced. The finish seemed to last forever. What a beautiful wine. For me, Rayas is definitely the most pure expression of Grenache.

Thanks for all the tips!

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Like Truchot on steroids. Is that a good thing?
